lens.included_activities: Independent US copper, brass, bronze, zinc, magnesium, titanium, and other nonaluminum nonferrous foundries outside die casting, in the approximately $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band, repeatedly producing customer-designed castings for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Aluminum foundries, die casters, captive foundries, establishments further manufacturing castings into their own finished products, primary smelters, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring engineered business-to-business casting programs priced per part or lot with pattern, tooling, alloy, testing, finishing, and metal-surcharge economics under quotes, releases, purchase orders, and supply agreements.

a.bridge: BLS foundry occupations and national casting-machine projections cover broader metals and processes rather than wage-weighted tasks in 331529. Physical molding, coremaking, melting, pouring, finishing, inspection, maintenance, and handling were largely excluded, while quoting, methoding support, documentation, scheduling, quality analysis, maintenance knowledge, and administration were partly exposed; specialty-alloy engineering may make the broader proxy understate information work.
a.rationale: Nonferrous foundry work is dominated by part-specific physical preparation and molten-metal processing. AI can assist information retrieval, analysis, and coordination, but replacing most production labor requires physical automation and capital rather than software alone.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from independent 331529 foundries by alloy and casting process, documenting existing simulation, ERP, molding, inspection, finishing, and maintenance automation.
caveats: OEWS data cover all foundries, not 331529.
Copper-alloy, magnesium, titanium, and specialty foundries may have different engineering and inspection intensity.

rho.rationale: Quote support, routing drafts, schedule assistance, defect-pattern analysis, quality records, maintenance retrieval, procurement, and administration are implementable over five years. Sparse part-level data, alloy-specific metallurgy, legacy systems, safety, export or customer requirements, traceability, and engineering approval constrain realization.
rho.change_evidence: Audited AI deployments in specialty nonferrous foundries reporting labor hours, quote time, scrap, yield, lead time, downtime, inspection accuracy, requalification, and human-review rates.
caveats: No representative LMM implementation study was found.
Conventional casting simulation and statistical process control already automate part of the analytical work.

e.rationale: Independent specialty nonferrous foundries commonly provide recurring outsourced castings and fit the operating lens. Captive plants, integrated finished-product producers, customer concentration, environmental exposure, obsolete equipment, key-person metallurgy, weak management depth, and size-band errors exclude a material share.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the frozen n population with alloy and process, captive status, external revenue, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, certifications, tooling ownership, environmental liabilities, equipment, and stand-alone management.
caveats: No direct eligible-firm denominator was found.
The injected n=78 uses a machinery margin bridge rather than a specialty-foundry margin.

s5.bridge: Census reports ages for responding employer-business owners across all industries rather than five-year 331529 control transfers. A portion of the 51% age-55-plus population plus ordinary strategic sales is translated into qualifying transfers after excluding continued ownership, family succession, closures, and nontransferable operations; this may overstate sales for family shops and understate strategic demand for scarce alloy capabilities.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership creates succession pressure, while scarce process knowledge, customer qualifications, patterns, and equipment support transfer value. Environmental diligence, customer approval, capital needs, and reliance on individual metallurgists can prevent completion.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year census of completed, failed, and withdrawn specialty nonferrous foundry transactions in the EBITDA band, paired with owner-age, succession, technical-staff retention, environmental, and customer-consent interviews.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is all-industry and data year 2018.
Owner age does not directly measure a firm's willingness or ability to transfer.

q.rationale: Specialty alloys, lower volumes, qualification, patterns, application knowledge, and limited alternative sources can protect benefits from yield, lead time, uptime, and quality. Metal-price pass-through, competitive quotes, customer cost-down requests, and rebids still share recurring gains.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract and program-margin sampling by alloy and end market, showing metal surcharges, open-book or cost-down terms, qualification, tooling ownership, rebid cadence, and margin persistence after productivity improvements.
caveats: No observed contract-level retention dataset was found.
Retention is likely lower for standardized copper-alloy castings and higher for highly qualified specialty components.

d5.bridge: BLS projects all-foundry real output declining 0.8% annually through 2034, while AFS projects one-year casting sales growth across all metals. Applying a near-flat five-year trajectory to 331529 may understate electrification, infrastructure, defense, and scarce-alloy demand or overstate it where imports, fabrication, machining, or additive processes replace castings.
d5.rationale: The broad foundry outlook is negative, but AFS projected positive 2025 casting sales and copper is indispensable in electrical equipment and infrastructure. Diverse specialty end markets support a near-flat central case rather than applying the full broad-industry decline mechanically.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year US shipment forecasts by copper alloy, magnesium, zinc, titanium, and other nonferrous casting process and end market, reconciled with customer awards, grid and defense investment, imports, substitution, and capacity closures.
caveats: AFS sales growth is nominal, one-year, and covers all metalcasting.
BLS output covers all foundries and is not physical 331529 constant-quality quantity.

o.rationale: Specialty nonferrous castings require accountable alloy control, mold preparation, melting, pouring, solidification, finishing, testing, traceability, and delivery. Software can improve tasks but cannot supply the physical casting or remove the responsible foundry operator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customer in-sourcing at scale, fabrication or additive processes broadly eliminating these castings, or autonomous specialty foundries operating without accountable production, metallurgy, and quality management.
caveats: Process and material substitution are captured principally in d5.
Digital interfaces can eliminate transactional labor without eliminating the operator.

narrative.executive_view: Other nonferrous foundries combine very high injected labor intensity, scarce metallurgy, qualified repeat programs, and a meaningful estimated LMM population. AI opportunity remains confined mainly to quoting, engineering support, quality analytics, maintenance knowledge, scheduling, and administration because core work is physical.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can retrieve comparable jobs, assist estimates and routings, analyze defect histories, prioritize maintenance, schedule work, and draft quality records. Molding, coremaking, alloying, melting, pouring, shakeout, finishing, test release, handling, and repair remain physical or human-supervised.
narrative.value_capture: Special alloys, qualifications, patterns, and scarce alternative suppliers can protect operational gains. Metal surcharges, rebids, and customer cost-down demands still distribute a meaningful share of recurring savings.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen estimate of 78 band firms depends on a machinery margin bridge. Captive status, environmental obligations, concentrated customers, equipment, management depth, and retention of metallurgical knowledge determine actual eligibility and transferability.
narrative.demand_durability: Electrification, infrastructure, defense, and specialty applications offset broad foundry contraction, while imports and process substitution remain threats. The result is a near-flat central demand case with large alloy- and customer-specific dispersion.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest gaps are alloy-level tasks and shipments, current automation, end-market exposure, import competition, customer contracts, environmental liabilities, key-person risk, and true EBITDA. A single qualification loss or alloy-market shock can dominate incremental AI gains.
narrative.principal_driver: High labor intensity and scarce, qualified specialty programs support AI assistance in quoting, quality, maintenance, engineering, and scheduling.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most labor is physical and alloy-specific, while the population is small and direct demand and transaction evidence are weak.

sources.title: NAICS 331529: Other Nonferrous Metal Foundries (except Die-Casting)
sources.supports: Census classifies establishments pouring nonaluminum nonferrous molten metal into molds without high-pressure die casting and distinguishes firms further manufacturing castings into finished products.

sources.title: Foundries - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: BLS provides employer-survey occupation and wage estimates for foundries across production, maintenance, engineering, management, and administrative work, used as the occupation-mix proxy.

sources.title: Occupational projections and worker characteristics, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS reports 154,600 molding, coremaking, and casting machine setters, operators, and tenders in 2024 and projects 148,800 in 2034, a 3.8% decline.

sources.title: Aluminum, Copper, and Other Nonferrous Foundries: NESHAP for Area Sources
sources.supports: EPA states that smaller aluminum, copper, and other nonferrous foundries are subject to air-toxics standards regulating metals including beryllium, cadmium, chromium, lead, manganese, and nickel.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey infographic reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were age 55 or older; estimates cover 4.1 million responding owners in data year 2018.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS projects foundry employment declining from 106,000 in 2024 to 90,400 in 2034 and real output declining from $26.9 billion to $24.8 billion, a 0.8% annual rate.

sources.title: AFS Projects 1.9 Percent Casting Sales Growth for 2025
sources.supports: The American Foundry Society projected 1.9% casting sales growth for 2025 in its Metalcasting Forecast & Trends release.
